chunks.t: HERE was once a velveteen rabbit,
and in the beginning he was really splendid.
his coat was spotted brown and white,
and his ears were lined with pink sateen.
in the top of the Boy's stocking,
with a sprig of holly between his paws,
There were other things in the stocking,
nuts and oranges and a toy engine,
and chocolate almonds and a clockwork mouse,
but the Rabbit was quite the best of all.
and then Aunts and Uncles came to dinner,
a great rustling of tissue paper and unwrapping of parcels,
and in the excitement of looking at all the new presents
he lived in the toy cupboard
and no one thought very much about him.
and being only made of velveteen,
some of the more expensive toys quite snubbed him.
The mechanical toys were very superior,
and looked down upon every one else;
they were full of modern ideas,
who had lived through two seasons
and lost most of his paint,
and never missed an opportunity of referring to his rigging
to be a model of anything,
he thought they were all stuffed with sawdust like himself,
and should never be mentioned in modern circles.
Even Timothy, the jointed wooden lion,
who was made by the disabled soldiers,
and should have had broader views,
and pretended he was connected with Government.
Between them all the poor little Rabbit was made
to feel himself very insignificant and commonplace,
who was kind to him at all
The Skin Horse had lived longer
in the nursery than any of the others.
that his brown coat was bald in patches
and most of the hairs in his tail
had been pulled out to string bead necklaces.
a long succession of mechanical toys arrive
and would never turn into anything else.
For nursery magic is very strange and wonderful,
the Skin Horse understand all about it.
"What is REAL?" asked the Rabbit one day,
by side near the nursery fender,
"Real isn't how you are made," said the Skin Horse.
"It's a thing that happens to you.
"Sometimes," said the Skin Horse, for he was always truthful.
"When you are Real you don't mind being hurt."
"It doesn't happen all at once," said the Skin Horse.
That's why it doesn't happen often to people
or who have to be carefully kept.
most of your hair has been loved off,
in the joints and very shabby.
But these things don't matter at all,
because once you are Real you can't be ugly,
"I suppose you are real?" said the Rabbit.
the Skin Horse might be sensitive.
But the Skin Horse only smiled.

sections.title: The Skin Horse Tells His Story

chunks.t: "The Boy's Uncle made me Real," he said.
"That was a great many years ago;
but once you are Real you can't become unreal again.
He thought it would be a long time
to know what it felt like; and yet
and losing his eyes and whiskers
He wished that he could become it
There was a person called Nana who ruled the nursery.
Sometimes she took no notice of the playthings lying about,
and sometimes, for no reason whatever, she went swooping about
and hustled them away in cupboards.
all hated it, especially the tin ones.
The Rabbit didn't mind it so much,
for wherever he was thrown he came down soft.
One evening, when the Boy was going to bed,
he couldn't find the china dog
and it was too much trouble
to hunt for china dogs at bedtime,
so she simply looked about her,
and seeing that the toy cupboard door stood open,
He'll do to sleep with you!"
And she dragged the Rabbit out
and put him into the Boy's arms.
That night, and for many nights after, the Velveteen Rabbit
for the Boy hugged him very tight,
and sometimes he rolled over on him,
and sometimes he pushed him so far
that the Rabbit could scarcely breathe.
those long moonlight hours in the nursery,
when all the house was silent,
and his talks with the Skin Horse.
But very soon he grew to like it,
for the Boy used to talk to him,
that he said were like the burrows
And they had splendid games together, in whispers,
when Nana had gone away to her supper
and left the night-light burning on the mantelpiece.
And when the Boy dropped off to sleep,
the Rabbit would snuggle down close
under his little warm chin and dream,
with the Boy's hands clasped close round him all night long.
and the little Rabbit was very happy-so happy
how his beautiful velveteen fur was getting shabbier and shabbier,
and all the pink rubbed off his nose
where the Boy had kissed him.
Spring came, and they had long days in the garden,
He had rides in the wheelbarrow,
for him under the raspberry canes behind the flower border.
And once, when the Boy was called away suddenly
on the lawn until long after dusk,
to come and look for him with the candle
because the Boy couldn't go to sleep
with the dew and quite earthy
the Boy had made for him
with a corner of her apron.
"Fancy all that fuss for a toy!"
The Boy sat up in bed and stretched out his hands.
that what the Skin Horse had said was true at last.
The nursery magic had happened to him,
and he was a toy no longer.
The Boy himself had said it.
That night he was almost too happy to sleep,
that had long ago lost their polish,
there came a look of wisdom and beauty,
so that even Nana noticed it next morning
if that old Bunny hasn't got
and in the long June evenings
the Boy liked to go there after tea to play.
He took the Velveteen Rabbit with him,
or play at brigands among the trees,
where he would be quite cosy,
for he was a kind-hearted little boy
and he liked Bunny to be comfortable.
One evening, while the Rabbit was lying there alone,
and fro between his velvet paws in the grass,
creep out of the tall bracken near him.
They were rabbits like himself, but quite furry and brand-new.
They must have been very well made,
for their seams didn't show at all,
and they changed shape in a queer way
one minute they were long and thin
and the next minute fat and bunchy,
instead of always staying the same
Their feet padded softly on the ground,
and they crept quite close to him,
to see which side the clockwork stuck out,
They were evidently a new kind of rabbit altogether.
They stared at him, and the little Rabbit stared back.
And all the time their noses twitched.
"Why don't you get up and play with us?"
to explain that he had no clockwork.
And he gave a big hop sideways
and stood on his hind legs.
He meant when the Boy threw him,
he didn't want to say so.
"Can you hop on your hind legs?" asked the furry rabbit.
The back of him was made all
in one piece, like a pincushion.
He sat still in the bracken,
and hoped that the other rabbits wouldn't notice.
But the wild rabbits have very sharp eyes.
And this one stretched out his neck and looked.
"Fancy a rabbit without any hind legs!"
And he began to whirl round and dance,
till the little Rabbit got quite dizzy.
But all the while he was longing to dance,
for a funny new tickly feeling ran through him,
he would give anything in the world
to be able to jump about
The strange rabbit stopped dancing, and came quite close.
He came so close this time
and then he wrinkled his nose suddenly
"I am Real!" said the little Rabbit.
And he nearly began to cry.
Just then there was a sound of footsteps,
and the Boy ran past near them,
of feet and a flash of white tails
"Come back and play with me!" called the little Rabbit.
only the little ants ran to and fro,
where the two strangers had passed.
The Velveteen Rabbit was all alone.
"Why did they run away like that?
Why couldn't they stop and talk to me?"
For a long time he lay very still,
and hoping that they would come back.
and presently the sun sank lower
and the little white moths fluttered out,
Weeks passed, and the little Rabbit grew very old and shabby,
but the Boy loved him just as much.
that he loved all his whiskers off,
turned grey, and his brown spots faded.
He even began to lose his shape,
and he scarcely looked like a rabbit any more,
To him he was always beautiful,
that the little Rabbit cared about.
how he looked to other people,
because the nursery magic had made him Real,
and when you are Real shabbiness
And then, one day, the Boy was ill.
and he talked in his sleep,
and his little body was so hot
and a light burned all night
all the little Velveteen Rabbit lay there,
hidden from sight under the bedclothes,
some one might take him away,
It was a long weary time,
for the Boy was too ill to play,
and looked forward to the time
when the Boy should be well again,
in the garden amongst the flowers
and play splendid games in the raspberry thicket
All sorts of delightful things he planned,
and while the Boy lay half asleep he crept up close
and whispered them in his ear.
And presently the fever turned, and the Boy got better.
He was able to sit up in bed
while the little Rabbit cuddled close
And one day, they let him get up and dress.
It was a bright, sunny morning, and the windows stood wide open.
They had carried the Boy out on
and the little Rabbit lay tangled up
The Boy was going to the seaside to-morrow.
to carry out the doctor's orders.
while the little Rabbit lay under the bedclothes,
with just his head peeping out, and listened.
The room was to be disinfected,
and toys that the Boy had played with in bed
"To-morrow we shall go to the seaside!"
For the boy had often talked
to see the big waves coming in,
and the tiny crabs, and the sand castles.
Just then Nana caught sight of him.
"How about his old Bunny?" she asked.
"Why, it's a mass of scarlet fever germs!-Burn it at once.
He mustn't have that any more!"
was put into a sack with the old picture-books
to the end of the garden behind the fowl-house.
only the gardener was too busy just then
to dig and the green peas to gather,
and he had a new bunny
all white plush with real glass eyes,
but the Boy was too excited
to care very much about it.
For to-morrow he was going to the seaside,
that he could think of nothing else.
And while the Boy was asleep,
lay among the old picture-books in the corner behind the fowl-house,
The sack had been left untied,
and so by wriggling a bit he was able
to get his head through the opening
for he had always been used
to sleeping in a proper bed,
his coat had worn so thin
Near by he could see the thicket of raspberry canes,
growing tall and close like a tropical jungle,
in whose shadow he had played
with the Boy on bygone mornings.
He thought of those long sunlit hours
in the garden-how happy they were-and a great sadness came over him.
each more beautiful than the other,
the fairy huts in the flower-bed,
the quiet evenings in the wood
when he lay in the bracken
and the little ants ran over his paws;
He thought of the Skin Horse,
so wise and gentle, and all
if it all ended like this?
And a tear, a real tear, trickled down
And then a strange thing happened.
For where the tear had fallen
a flower grew out of the ground,
a mysterious flower, not at all like any
It had slender green leaves the colour of emeralds,
and in the centre of the leaves a blossom
that the little Rabbit forgot to cry,
and just lay there watching it.
opened, and out of it there stepped a fairy.
She was quite the loveliest fairy in the whole world.
Her dress was of pearl and dew-drops,
like the most perfect flower of all.
And she came close to the little Rabbit
and gathered him up in her arms
and kissed him on his velveteen nose
that was all damp from crying.
"Little Rabbit," she said, "don't you know who I am?"
The Rabbit looked up at her,
that he had seen her face before,
"I am the nursery magic Fairy," she said.
"I take care of all the playthings that the children have loved.
and the children don't need them any more,
and take them away with me
"Wasn't I Real before?" asked the little Rabbit.
Now you shall be Real to every one."
And she held the little Rabbit close
and flew with him into the wood.
It was light now, for the moon had risen.
and the fronds of the bracken shone like frosted silver.
In the open glade between the tree-trunks
danced with their shadows on the velvet grass,
the Fairy they all stopped dancing
and stood round in a ring
"I've brought you a new playfellow," the Fairy said.
"You must be very kind to him
he needs to know in Rabbit-land,
for he is going to live with you
And she kissed the little Rabbit again
and put him down on the grass.
But the little Rabbit sat quite still
all the wild rabbits dancing around him
he suddenly remembered about his hind legs,
that he was made all in one piece.
that when the Fairy kissed him
that last time she had changed him altogether.
And he might have sat there a long time,
if just then something hadn't tickled his nose,
And he found that he actually had hind legs!
Instead of dingy velveteen he had
and his whiskers were so long
and the joy of using those hind legs
that when at last he did stop
He was a Real Rabbit at last,
at home with the other rabbits.
when the days grew warm and sunny,
to play in the wood behind the house.
two rabbits crept out from the bracken
One of them was brown all over,
but the other had strange markings
as though long ago he had been spotted,
and the spots still showed through.
And about his little soft nose
so that the Boy thought to himself:
"Why, he looks just like my old Bunny
that it really was his own Bunny,
come back to look at the child
